content.text: Create an Agent with subagent_type "statusline-setup" and the prompt "Configure my statusLine from my shell PS1 configuration"

input.prompt: Configure my statusLine from my shell PS1 configuration

content.text:
Done. Here is a summary of what was configured:

**Script created:** `/home/garrosh/.claude/statusline-command.sh`

**Settings updated:** `/home/garrosh/.claude/settings.json`

The status line mirrors your p10k prompt elements using the same Naysayer color palette:

- `user@host` in linefg (`#126367`)
- Current working directory in blue (`#66D9EF`), with `~` abbreviation for home
- Git branch in linefg, with a `*` suffix in warn orange (`#ffaa00`) when the working tree is dirty
- Current time (`HH:MM:SS`) in fg (`#d0b892`)
- Claude model name in linefg
- Context usage percentage, color-coded: linefg below 50%, orange from 50-79%, red at 80%+

The prompt character (`λ`) and virtualenv segment were omitted since those are interactive shell concepts that don't apply to the Claude Code status line.

---

For any future changes to the status line — such as adding rate limit display, adjusting colors, or reordering segments — ask Claude to use the **statusline-setup** agent again, which will update `/home/garrosh/.claude/statusline-command.sh` and `/home/garrosh/.claude/settings.json` accordingly.

A sua status line foi configurada com sucesso! Ela espelha os elementos do seu prompt p10k usando a paleta de cores Naysayer:

- **user@host** em linefg (`#126367`)
- **Diretório atual** em azul (`#66D9EF`), com `~` para o home

O script está em `~/.claude/statusline-command.sh` e as configurações foram atualizadas em `~/.claude/settings.json`.

message.content: adicione uma usage bar and context bar in the claude statusline

input.prompt:
Update the existing statusline configuration at /home/garrosh/.claude/statusline-command.sh to add:

1. A **usage bar** - a visual progress bar showing API usage/cost percentage using block characters (▏▎▍▌▋▊▉█) that fills up as usage increases. Color it with the Naysayer palette: linefg (#126367) when low, warn orange (#ffaa00) when medium (50-79%), and red when high (80%+).

2. A **context bar** - a visual progress bar showing context window usage percentage using the same block character style. Same color coding as above.

Both bars should be compact (around 10 characters wide) and show the percentage next to them. Read the existing script first to understand the current format and integrate these bars naturally into the existing statusline layout.

content.text:
Everything looks correct. Here is a summary of what was changed in `/home/garrosh/.claude/statusline-command.sh`.

---

**What was added**

A `make_bar` function (lines 17-64) that:

- Takes a percentage and an optional width (default 10 characters).
- Computes fill using sub-character precision with the eight-step block sequence `▏▎▍▌▋▊▉█`, so the bar animates smoothly rather than jumping in full-block steps.
- Applies Naysayer palette colors automatically:
  - 0-49% — linefg `#126367`
  - 50-79% — warn orange `#ffaa00`
  - 80-100% — red `#ff0000`
- Renders as `[██████▌   ] 68%` (brackets + bar + space + percentage).

**Two new segments after the model name**

- `api:[bar] N%` — drawn from `.rate_limits.five_hour.used_percentage`; only appears after the first API response when Claude.ai subscription rate-limit data is available.
- `ctx:[bar] N%` — drawn from `.context_window.used_percentage`; only appears once at least one API call has been made in the session.

Both are omitted entirely when their data is absent (e.g., before the first message), so the status line stays clean at startup.

The `settings.json` at `/home/garrosh/.claude/settings.json` was not changed — the existing `"command": "bash /home/garrosh/.claude/statusline-command.sh"` entry already points to the right script.

---

For any further changes to the status line, use the **statusline-setup** agent — you can ask Claude to adjust colors, add new segments, reorder elements, or tweak bar widths at any time.

---

A `make_bar` function (lines 17-64) that:

- Takes a percentage and an optional width (default 10 characters).
- Computes fill using sub-character precision with the eight-step block sequence `▏▎▍▌▋▊▉█`, so the bar animates smoothly rather than jumping in full-block steps.
- Applies Naysayer palette colors automatically:
  - 0-49% — linefg `#126367`
  - 50-79% — warn orange `#ffaa00`
  - 80-100% — red `#ff0000`
- Renders as `[██████▌   ] 68%` (brackets + bar + space + percentage).

**Two new segments after the model name**

- `api:[bar] N%` — drawn from `.rate_limits.five_hour.used_percentage`; only appears after the first API response when Claude.ai subscription rate-limit data is available.

As barras foram adicionadas à sua statusline! Agora você tem:

- **`api:[██████▌   ] 68%`** — barra visual do uso da API (rate limit de 5h), com precisão sub-caractere usando blocos Unicode (▏▎▍▌▋▊▉█)
- **`ctx:[██████▌   ] 68%`** — barra visual do uso da janela de contexto, mesmo estilo

Ambas usam cores dinâmicas da paleta Naysayer:
- **Verde** (`#126367`) abaixo de 50%
- **Laranja** (`#ffaa00`) entre 50-79%
- **Vermelho** (`#ff0000`) acima de 80%

As barras só aparecem após a primeira interação com a API, mantendo a statusline limpa no início da sessão.

message.content: remove the @host name, let only my user name in status line

input.prompt: Update the existing statusline script at /home/garrosh/.claude/statusline-command.sh to remove the @hostname from the user@host segment. It should show only the username (e.g., "garrosh" instead of "garrosh@hostname"). Read the script first to find the exact line to change.

content.text:
Done. Line 67 in `/home/garrosh/.claude/statusline-command.sh` has been updated:

- Before: `printf '\033[38;2;18;99;103m%s@%s\033[0m' "$(whoami)" "$(hostname -s)"`
- After: `printf '\033[38;2;18;99;103m%s\033[0m' "$(whoami)"`

The status line will now show just the username (e.g., `garrosh`) instead of `garrosh@hostname`. The color remains unchanged.

Note: for any further status line changes, the `statusline-setup` agent should be used. You can also just ask Claude directly to make additional tweaks and it will handle them for you.

input.content:
---
name: Open-ended questions in plan mode
description: Always ask open-ended questions when in plan mode to gather more context and explore possibilities
type: feedback
---

Ask open-ended questions when in plan mode instead of closed/yes-no questions.

**Why:** Open-ended questions lead to richer context, better understanding of requirements, and surface edge cases or preferences the user might not mention otherwise.

**How to apply:** When entering plan mode or discussing approach/architecture, frame questions broadly (e.g., "What are your thoughts on..." / "How do you envision..." / "What constraints should I consider...") rather than narrow yes/no questions (e.g., "Should I use X?").
